techniques.comment: Sysmon Event ID 10 (process access to lsass.exe) is a mature, widely-used detection pattern most SIEMs ship built-in.
No telemetry targets browser cookie database access specifically; would need EDR-level file monitoring.
Passive sniffing doesn't generate host-based log events; would need network-level (NDR) monitoring, not in this telemetry set.
Passive sniffing doesn't generate host-based log events; would need network-level (NDR) monitoring, not in this telemetry set.
Sysmon logs process access to browser/vault files, but no tuned rule exists for known credential-store paths yet.
Command-line credential exposure is visible via Sysmon process creation logs, but no active scan for creds in files/scripts
4769 events are logged, but no tuned rule exists for encryption-downgrade (Kerberoasting) or anomalous TGT lifetimes.
Classic brute force caught via 4625 threshold rule, but slow password-spray attempts would likely evade it.
Keylogging via API hooking evades standard Windows Security logs; needs EDR behavioral monitoring on hook APIs.
Keylogging via API hooking evades standard Windows Security logs; needs EDR behavioral monitoring on hook APIs.
Not visible in Windows host logs; requires identity provider (e.g., Entra ID) sign-in log analysis, which isn't in scope yet.
Registry keys tied to auth package/SSP modification aren't monitored today; needs a purpose-built registry-value detection.
Registry keys tied to auth package/SSP modification aren't monitored today; needs a purpose-built registry-value detection.
Registry keys tied to auth package/SSP modification aren't monitored today; needs a purpose-built registry-value detection.
